title: Dynamic Routing: Capturing Dynamic Segments in the URL

content.problemStatement:
In this lesson, we'll explore dynamic routing in Flask, which allows us to capture variable parts of a URL. This is a powerful feature that builds upon the basic routing we learned in our first Flask application.

Dynamic routing enables us to create more flexible and reusable routes by allowing parts of the URL to be variable. These variable parts are called dynamic segments.

Let's say we want to create a route that displays a user's profile. Instead of creating a separate route for each user, we can use a dynamic segment to capture the username from the URL.

Here's how we can define a route with a dynamic segment:

```python
@app.route('/user/')
def user_profile(username):
    return f"This is the profile page for {username}"
```

In this example:
- The `` in the route is a dynamic segment.
- It captures whatever value is in that part of the URL.
- The captured value is passed as an argument to the function.

So, if a user visits "/user/john", the `username` parameter in the function will be "john".

You can have multiple dynamic segments in a single route:

```python
@app.route('/blog///')
def blog_post(year, month, title):
    return f"Blog post: {title} (Posted: {month}/{year})"
```

This route would match URLs like "/blog/2023/5/my-first-post".

Dynamic routing allows you to create more flexible and powerful applications by capturing and using parts of the URL in your view functions. This concept builds on the basic routing we learned earlier and opens up many possibilities for creating dynamic web applications with Flask.
